title: How to Rent a Boat in the Canary Islands: A Practical Guide
topic: How to rent a boat in the Canary Islands
html:
The Canary Islands sit in the Atlantic off the northwest coast of Africa, and their volcanic coastlines, year-round mild weather, and calm leeward bays make them one of the most rewarding places in Europe to spend a day on the water. Whether you fancy spotting dolphins off Tenerife, drifting past the cliffs of La Gomera, or anchoring in a quiet cove on Lanzarote, renting a boat is the surest way to see the archipelago at its best. This guide walks through how to pick the right island, the right vessel, and the right booking so the day runs smoothly.

Pick your island and the right side of it

Each of the seven main islands has its own character on the water. Tenerife is the busiest hub, with whale and dolphin trips leaving daily from the south coast around Los Cristianos and Costa Adeje. Gran Canaria offers sheltered marinas and easy day cruising near Puerto Rico and Mogan. Lanzarote and Fuerteventura reward those chasing turquoise shallows and steady breezes, while La Palma, La Gomera, and El Hierro suit travellers who want quieter, more dramatic scenery.

One local detail matters everywhere: the wind. The prevailing trade winds blow from the northeast, so the southern and southwestern coasts are usually calmest and best for relaxed outings. If you want flat water for swimming and snorkelling, plan to stay on the leeward side and check the forecast the evening before.

Choose a boat that matches the day you want

Start with the experience, then match the vessel to it. A short trip of swimming, sunbathing, and coastal sightseeing suits a motorboat with a shaded deck and a swim ladder. A larger group or a full-day cruise is often more comfortable on a catamaran, which gives you space and a steady ride even when the Atlantic swell picks up.

  • Motorboats and RIBs: quick and nimble, ideal for reaching dolphin grounds and hidden coves in a hurry.
  • Sailing yachts: perfect for travellers who want to use those reliable trade winds and cover real distance under sail.
  • Catamarans: spacious and stable, a favourite for families, parties, and anyone planning a longer day aboard.

When you rent a boat through a marketplace, you can filter by size, type, and whether a crew is included, so the options that appear already fit what you have in mind.

Skippered or self-drive

Most Canary rentals come in two forms. A skippered charter includes a local captain who knows the currents around the headlands, the best anchorages, and where the dolphins tend to gather. It is the easy and safe choice if you do not hold a licence or do not know these waters. A bareboat charter puts you at the helm, but operators will usually ask to see a recognised qualification and may give a short handover before you leave the dock.

If it is your first time in the Atlantic, a skipper is money well spent. You relax, learn the coast, and leave the navigation to someone who does it every day.

A clear listing on a good boat rental platform will always state whether a vessel is offered with crew or without, along with any experience the owner requires.

Understand the costs and what is included

Prices depend on the boat, the season, and the length of the trip. Summer and the Christmas and Easter holidays are the busiest and dearest periods, so booking ahead pays off. Beyond the headline rate, check exactly what is bundled in. Some prices include fuel, insurance, and safety gear, while others add fuel or a cleaning fee separately. For crewed trips it is normal to budget for a tip and to confirm whether drinks and snacks are provided.

Always read the cancellation terms, the security deposit, and the number of hours included before you confirm. Booking a yacht charter through a trusted platform keeps all of this in one place, so there are no surprises on the morning of your trip.

Book safely and prepare for the day

Choose a marketplace that verifies its operators and shows genuine reviews from past guests. Confirm the meeting point, the start time, and what to bring. High-factor sunscreen, a hat, plenty of water, and a dry bag for phones are sensible for any Canary outing, since the Atlantic sun is strong even on breezy days. Arrive a little early so the safety briefing is unhurried, and listen carefully before you cast off. When you are ready, compare crewed and self-drive options across the islands and find a boat that suits your group and budget.

For boat owners

If you keep a vessel in the Canaries and it sits idle between outings, you can list your boat and reach travellers from around the world who are searching for exactly the kind of day you can offer.
